name: The sending of the Twelve
notes: A threefold synoptic parallel of the mission charge Jesus gave to His twelve apostles (Matt 10:1-15; Mark 6:7-13; Luke 9:1-6), distinct from the later sending of the seventy in Luke 10. All three accounts share the core: He called the Twelve and gave them power over unclean spirits and to heal, sent them forth (Mark adding 'by two and two'), charged them to take nothing for the journey, told them to abide in one house till they departed, and to shake off the dust of their feet for a testimony against those who received them not. Each evangelist preserves his own vantage: Matthew expands the charge into a long discourse (beyond v.15, out of scope) and records the apostles' names and the limit to the lost sheep of Israel; Mark and Luke alone narrate the apostles going out, preaching, and healing everywhere. The wording on the staff and sandals differs between the accounts (Mark permits a staff and sandals; Matthew and Luke list staves among what not to provide); these complementary vantages are left as a matter for further study.
